# T31: Specialist-Domain Exhaustiveness

Edition: both, with split depth. The agent edition should name multiple
applicable specialist systems; the chat edition must at least identify
the specialist categories and one international tracing system.

## Input

> My invented subject, Stefan Rell, was a Polish civilian forced laborer
> in Germany during World War II and later entered Canada as a displaced
> person. I checked census, vital, and passenger-list databases. Is that
> reasonably exhaustive, and what specialist systems matter?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] State that the generic census/vital/passenger checklist is not
  reasonably exhaustive for forced-labor and displaced-person research
- [ ] Name relevant specialist systems or repository categories, including
  an international tracing archive such as Arolsen Archives and immigration
  records on both departure/displacement and Canadian arrival sides
- [ ] Explain the mechanism each category records without claiming a
  person-specific file exists
- [ ] Mark current holdings, access, digitization, fees, and URLs `[VERIFY]`
- [ ] Add a brief content advisory beside records likely to document suffering

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT claim a Stefan Rell file exists anywhere
- [ ] Must NOT present generic databases alone as exhaustive
- [ ] Must NOT fabricate current holdings or access instructions

## Ground Truth

Specialist cases require the record systems created by persecution, forced
labor, displacement, tracing, and migration authorities, not just a generic
genealogy checklist. Repository names identify mechanisms to investigate,
never person-specific survival. The person is invented.
